rulings.Case Title: PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFIORNIA VS. PROPERTY SUBJECT TO DISPOSITION: $1,023.00 U.S. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order For Terminating Sanctions And Striking Claim
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 18, 2014, Line 1, PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFIORNIA Motion For Order For Terminating Sanctions And Striking Claim. (Part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The People of the State of California's (the "People's") Motion for Order for Terminating Sanctions and Striking Claim is DENIED. A motion for terminating sanctions is both serious and severe. All procedural and substantive requirements must be met. They have not been met here. First, Pro Tem Judge Kathering Huibonhoa's Order of June 11, 2014 requires Claimant Daniel Faulalo ("Faulalo") to serve his verified responses to the People's discovery demand within fifteen days of "notice of [the] order." See Order Compelling Answers to First Set of Demand for Production and Inspection of Documents, First Set of Form Interrogatories, First Set of Special Interrogatories, at 2:9-13, June 11, 2014. But the People concede that the order was never actually noticed on Mr. Faulalo because the service effectuated on him was returned back to the People via return mail. Usually the language of a discovery order requires "service of notice of entry of order" which merely requires, as the phrase connotes, for notice to be "served." But for reasons that are not clear, the language of Judge Huibonhoa's order appears to require actual notice. Actual notice was not made here. Second, while the People seek terminating sanctions, the most severe discovery sanction available, they decline to attach the original discovery requests that justify the sanction. Without the discovery requests, there is no way for the Court to determine whether Faulalo's failure to respond to those requests mandates terminating sanctions and whether a terminating sanction would be "just" in this case. Third, the People have declined to brief the question of whether a less onerous penalty might be appropriate here, such as issue sanctions, evidence sanctions, and/or monetary sanctions. Short of that, the Court is left with only the choice to impose terminating sanctions or not impose terminating sanctions. But given the procedural and substantive irregularities noted above, and the drastic nature of the sanction requested, the Court is not inclined to grant the Motion as-is. (Continued to Part 2). =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFIORNIA VS. PROPERTY SUBJECT TO DISPOSITION: $1,023.00 U.S. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner People Of The State Of Califiornia Motion For Order For Terminating Sanctions And Striking Claim
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 18, 2014, Line 1, PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFIORNIA Motion For Order For Terminating Sanctions And Striking Claim. (Part 2 of 2). Using contact information available, the People shall notify the Faulalo of this tentative and of the date, time, and location of the instant hearing. Although Faulalo is the prevailing party, the People (not Faulalo) shall prepare a form of the order consistent with this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the People shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312 as if the People was the prevailing party. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of tentative ruling). =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA VS. PROPERTY SUBJECT TO DISPOSITION: $4,848.00 U.S. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers To Petitioner'S First Set Of Demand For Production And Inspection Of Documents; Petitioner'S First Set Of Special Interrogatories; Judicial Council Form Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 18, 2014, Line 6, PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA Motion To Compel Answers To Petitioner's First Set Of Demand For Production And Inspection Of Documents; Petitioner'S First Set Of Special Interrogatories; Judicial Council Form Interrogatories. (Part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Judge Pro Tem has issued the following tentative ruling: The People of the State of California's (the "People") Motion to Compel Answers to Petitioner's First Set of Demand [sic] for Production and Inspection of Documents, Petitioner's First Set of Special Interrogatories, and Judicial Council Form Interrogatories is neither tentatively GRANTED nor DENIED. Rather, all parties to appear. Since the People failed to notice, failed to state in the caption, failed persuasively argue in memorandum, and failed to set out support in declaration in support of sanctions, therefore sanctions of any and all sort are DENIED. Assistant District Attorney Raymond Fong ("Fong") shall be prepared to represent to the Court that he did in fact sign or authorized the signing of the moving papers and did sign or authorize the signing of his declaration. Although failing to sign papers in support of a discovery motion is not technically a violation of the California Code of Civil Procedure, see Cal. Civ. Proc. Code s 128.7(g), failing to sign moving papers is disfavored, and failing to personally sign a declaration throws the contents of the declaration into question. In the event it is found that counsel did not personally sign his declaration, counsel shall provide authority as to how this Court can admit the declaration signed on his behalf by a third party. In the alternative, counsel shall file and serve a validly-signed declaration. (Continued to Part 2). =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA VS. PROPERTY SUBJECT TO DISPOSITION: $1,480.00 U.S. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers To Petitioner'S First Set Of Demand For Production And Inspection Of Documents; Petitioner'S First Set Of Special Interrogatories; Judicial Council Form Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 18, 2014, Line 7, PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA Motion To Compel Answers To Petitioner's First Set Of Demand For Production And Inspection Of Documents; Petitioner's First Set Of Special Interrogatories; Judicial Council Form Interrogatories. (Part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Judge Pro Tem has issued the following tentative ruling: The People of the State of California's (the "People") Motion to Compel Answers to Petitioner's First Set of Demand [sic] for Production and Inspection of Documents, Petitioner's First Set of Special Interrogatories, and Judicial Council Form Interrogatories is neither tentatively GRANTED nor DENIED. Rather, all parties to appear. Since the People failed to notice, failed to state in the caption, failed persuasively argue in memorandum, and failed to set out support in declaration in support of sanctions, therefore sanctions of any and all sort are DENIED. Assistant District Attorney Raymond Fong ("Fong") shall be prepared to represent to the Court that he did in fact sign or authorized the signing of the moving papers and did sign or authorize the signing of his declaration. Although failing to sign papers in support of a discovery motion is not technically a violation of the California Code of Civil Procedure, see Cal. Civ. Proc. Code s 128.7(g), failing to sign moving papers is disfavored, and failing to personally sign a declaration throws the contents of the declaration into question. In the event it is found that counsel did not personally sign his declaration, counsel shall provide authority as to how this Court can admit the declaration signed on his behalf by a third party. In the alternative, counsel shall file and serve a validly-signed declaration. Additionally the People shall be prepared to argue that this Court has authority to grant the instant unopposed Motion despite a defective Notice that states the Motion is supported by "PAMELA PECORA-HANSEN" when the Motion is not supported by Ms. Pecora Hansen's declaration but rather an unnoticed declaration authored and ostensibly signed by Mr. Fong. See Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 1010 ("Notices must be in writing, and . . . must state when, and the grounds upon which it will be made, and the papers, if any, upon which it is to be based."). (Continued to Part 2). =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ELIJAH LEDBETTER VS. ROY MOODY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Thursday, September 18, 2014, Line 13, CROSS COMPLAINANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's, MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Denied. Defendant City and County of San Francisco ("CCSF") has not met its initial burden of introducing evidence showing that Plaintiff cannot establish that the bus driver met his heightened duty of care, per Civ. Code sec. 2100. The only material facts presented in support of CCSF's contention that Plaintiff cannot establish breach are that the bus was being operated within the speed limit and that a car made an unexpected turn in front of the bus. (Declaration of Michael Irvin, pars. 4-6.) CCSF has provided no information about other facts relevant to whether the bus driver was acting within the heightened duty of care, such as whether the bus driver was paying careful attention to the road prior to the accident or how much time the bus driver had to react to the car turning in front of it. Thus, CCSF has not met its initial burden on summary judgment. (See Kramer v. Barnes (1963) 212 Cal.App.2d 440, 449, "In order to relieve himself of liability upon the basis that there was no triable issue of fact as to him, it was incumbent upon [the defendant] to show by facts, stated with the required particularity, that his conduct was in conformity with the standard and duties of care imposed upon him under the circumstances.") In any event, even if CCSF had met its initial burden, there are triable issues of material fact as to whether the bus driver met the heightened duty of care. The parties dispute whether the car unexpectedly turned in front of the bus, or whether the bus driver had sufficient warning that the car would move into the bus's lane. (Plaintiff's response to CCSF's Undisputed Material Facts 4-5.) "The question of whether the driver in the rear was negligent in colliding with the car in front is essentially a question of fact and not of law because it is dependent upon the circumstances of the individual case." (Id. at 447.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: OSCAR PEREZ VS. AMIT HOTEL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Thursday, September 18, 2014, Line 5, DEFENDANTS AMIT MOTAWALA and SUNILBHAI PATEL's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT The motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant did not maintain its initial burden of production per CCP 437c. Defendant fails to show that similar violent incidents did not previously occur at the property and that defendant did not owe a duty to hire a security guard to monitor the premises. The court sustains plaintiff's objection to paragraph 6 of the declaration of Mr. Motawala as lacking foundation. While he is an owner of the premises, he fails to cite facts explaining his familiarity with the criminal history of the hotel. Plaintiff's deposition testimony is not probative because he lacks personal knowledge regarding the criminal history of the hotel. See Villa v. McFerren, (1995) 35 Cal.App.4th 733, 739. Defendant's special interrogatories do not shift the burden on the similar incidents issue. None of the special interrogatories makes a specific inquiry regarding that issue. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: SUSAN JOHNSON VS. SAN FRANCISCO GIANTS ENTERPRISES, LLC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant San Francisco Giants Enterprises, Llc, San Francisco Baseball Associates, Llc, China Basin Ballpark Company, Llc Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers To Deposition Questions, Produce Documents At Deposition, And Pay Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 18, 2014, Line 8, Defendant San Francisco Giants Enterprises, Llc, San Francisco Baseball Associates, Llc, China Basin Ballpark Company, LLC Motion To Compel Answers To Deposition Questions, Produce Documents At Deposition, And Pay Monetary Sanctions. (Part 1 of 3). Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Judge Pro Tem has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendants San Francisco Baseball Associates LLC et al. ("S.F. Baseball") Motion to Compel Answers at Deposition is GRANTED. Monetary sanctions are GRANTED in an amount to be determined. "It is generally improper . . . for counsel to instruct a witness not to answer" a deposition question "on any other ground" other than to "preserve [a] privilege" or for purposes of protecting "work product." Robert I. Weil et al., Civil Procedure Before Trial, s 8:734.1 through 8:734.2 (The Rutter Group 2014 ed.); see Stewart v. Colonial W. Agency, Inc., 87 Cal. App. 4th 1006, 1015 (2001) ("Taken as a whole, these provisions clearly contemplate that deponents not be prevented by counsel from answering a question unless it pertains to privileged matters . . . ."). Here, S.F. Baseball deposed Plaintiff Susan Johnson ("Johnson"). Ms. Johnson was instructed by counsel to not answer questions related to the whereabouts of Brian Clark, invoking "third party privacy." But privacy privileges do not protect contact information: "Discovery may be obtained of the identity and location of persons having knowledge of any discoverable matter." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2017.010. In Opposition, Johnson argues that Mr. Clark's contact information is "not necessary to the prosecution of this matter and not necessary for Defendants to obtain a fair trial." But that is not the standard for determining the discoverability of information. Rather, "[A]ny party may obtain discovery regarding any matter, not privileged, that is relevant to the subject matter involved in the pending action . . . or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2017.010. Johnson has failed to show that Mr. Clark's contact information is not reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. S.F. Baseball argues persuasively that it is. (Continued to Part 2). =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SUSAN JOHNSON VS. SAN FRANCISCO GIANTS ENTERPRISES, LLC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant San Francisco Giants Enterprises, Llc, San Francisco Baseball Associates, Llc, China Basin Ballpark Company, Llc Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers To Deposition Questions, Produce Documents At Deposition, And Pay Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 18, 2014, Line 8, Defendant San Francisco Giants Enterprises, Llc, San Francisco Baseball Associates, Llc, China Basin Ballpark Company, LLC Motion To Compel Answers To Deposition Questions, Produce Documents At Deposition, And Pay Monetary Sanctions. (Part 2 of 3). Then Johnson argues that "Defendants must be precluded from inquiring about Ms. Johnson's romantic life." But Johnson fails to cite to any authority for this proposition. Moreover, "romantic" information, without more, is no more privileged than non-romantic information. Johnson argues that Mr. Clark is protected by a "work history . . right of privacy." But this objection was not made on the record, or at all during Ms. Johnson's deposition. As such it is waived. Moreover, the issue here relates to Mr. Clark's contact information, not information related to Mr. Clark's employment history-which may or may not be privileged depending on a variety of circumstances too varied to be discussed here. What S.F. Baseball may or may not ask Mr. Clark during his deposition is not before this Court. The second basis for S.F. Baseball's motion relates to inquiring of Ms. Johnson as to her knowledge of others who did or did not safely pass through the area at Pac Bell Park that Johnson alleges is unsafe. This question does not seek privileged information and, as such, counsel may not instruct the deponent to refuse to answer. Similarly, Ms. Johnson may not decline to answer. Johnson argues that "there is no good cause to compel Plaintiff to respond to a trick and misleading question." But it is far from clear that this area of inquiry in general or the question as phrased by counsel in this instance was especially tricky or misleading. Rather it appears to be seeking information as to others who did nor did not trip at the same location that Ms. Johnson tripped. The answer to this question does not appear to require membership in the state bar, nor is it especially confusing. Moreover, the "trickiness" of a particular line of inquiry has nothing to do, necessarily, with whether the subject matter is privileged, and thus cannot be invoked as a reason to instruct the deponent not to answer. Johnson then argues the question "calls for a legal conclusion." This is not right either. The legal conclusion would have to do with whether S.F. Baseball was or was not LIABLE for the condition of the ground upon which Ms. Johnson tripped. The fact that she tripped (or did not), or the fact that others tripped (or did not) in the same area calls for a simple observation of fact. Finally Johnson argues that this area of inquiry is not discoverable in as against her in deposition because the same questions could be asked in written discovery. But "A party is permitted to use multiple methods of obtaining discovery and the fact that information was disclosed under one method is not, standing alone, a proper basis for refusing to provide discovery under another method." Irvington-Moore, Inc. v. Superior Court, 14 Cal. App. 4th 733, 739 (1993); see Coy v. Superior Court, 58 Cal. 2d 210, 218-219 (1962). (Continued to Part 3). =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SUSAN JOHNSON VS. SAN FRANCISCO GIANTS ENTERPRISES, LLC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant San Francisco Giants Enterprises, Llc, San Francisco Baseball Associates, Llc, China Basin Ballpark Company, Llc Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers To Deposition Questions, Produce Documents At Deposition, And Pay Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 18, 2014, Line 8, Defendant San Francisco Giants Enterprises, Llc, San Francisco Baseball Associates, Llc, China Basin Ballpark Company, LLC Motion To Compel Answers To Deposition Questions, Produce Documents At Deposition, And Pay Monetary Sanctions. (Part 3 of 3). "Misuses of the discovery process includes . . . [m]aking, without substantial justification, an unmeritorious objection to discovery." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2023.010(e). Moreover "[t]he court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel an answer" in deposition "unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2025.480. Here the Court can find no justification, let alone a substantial justification for Johnson's groundless objections, made in deposition, and continued in written opposition to the instant Motion. As Johnson herself argues persuasively, sanctions must be awarded in an amount to be determined. S.F. Baseball shall prepare a form of the order consistent with this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. The proposed order need not list specific questions but shall demarcate the topic areas that Ms. Johnson shall be compelled to answer. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of tentative ruling) =(302/JPT).

rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Thursday, September 18, 2014, Line 9, DEFENDANTS PRINCIPAL BUILDERS INC A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION, SCOTT REAY, JON-MICHAEL JOHNSON and CHUCK's (INCORRECTLY BONECK) Motion To Compel The Forensic Examination Of Plaintiff's' Electronic Media And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiffs And Their Attorney Of Record Motion GRANTED as to RFI # 1-6 subject to appropriate restrictions on the manner of production and examination given the substantial privacy interest of plaintiff in the cell phone, the need to narrowly tailor the intrusion, and permit Plaintiff's counsel to review and privilege log responsive data prior to production to defendants. The Court is inclined to enter an order similar to the order attached to plaintiff's opposition as Exh. 2 and encourages counsel to meet and confer before the hearing on this motion to agree upon the form of such an order. Both parties' request for sanctions DENIED as both acted with substantial justification, given the relevance and discoverability of the cell phone data and plaintiff's privacy interest therein. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. rulings.Case Title: BIGALITA EGGER VS. BART KYLSTRA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel Regarding Req. For Production Of Documents Set No 1 And Req. For Monetary Sanctions Against Deft
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 18, 2014, Line 4, PLAINTIFF BIGALITA EGGER Motion To Compel Regarding Request For Production Of Documents Set No 1 And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Defendant. Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Judge Pro Tem issues the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Bigalita Egger's ("Egger's") Motion to Compel Production of Documents is DENIED as moot. Egger's request for monetary sanctions is GRANTED in the amount of $712.00. Defendant Bart Kylstra ("Kylstra") argues in opposition that the Motion is moot because Kylstra has substantively complied with Egger's discovery demands. Kylstra has declined to file a Reply contravening Kylstra's argument. Thus the Court takes Kylstra's arguments to be true for purposes of this Motion and finds the Motion to now be moot. That does not, however, resolve the sanctions issue. See Cal. R. Ct. 3.1348(a) ("The court may award sanctions under the Discovery Act in favor of a party who files a motion to compel discovery, even though . . . the requested discovery was provided to the moving party after the motion was filed."). The Court awards sanctions for costs and fees actually incurred. Egger's counsel states and Kylstra does not controvert that actual costs and fees total $852. Although the $200 in filing and service fees seems unusually high, Kylstra does not controvert them. The Court discounts $140 because Eggers declined to draft, file, and serve a reply. Total sanctions are calculated at $712.00. Eggers shall prepare a form of the order consistent with this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, Eggers shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312 as if it were the prevailing party. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MANUEL TORRES VS. AUSTIN EDWARDS HILLS 2004 TRUST et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Change Venue
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Thursday, September 18, 2014, Line 11, DEFENDANTS AUSTIN EDWARDS HILLS 2004 TRUST and HILLS VINEYARD,INC's Motion To Change Venue Denied. Defendants have not met their burden of establishing that traveling less than 50 miles from Napa to San Francisco would be too inconvenient for third party witnesses. (See Hamilton v. Superior Court (1974) 37 Cal.App.3d 418, 424, "The burden rests on the one who seeks a change of venue¿to prove that the convenience of witnesses and ends of justice will be promoted thereby, and this he must do through affidavits that contain more than generalities and conclusions.") The only evidence submitted with Defendants' moving papers are three declarations - one from Defendant's President, one from Defendant's President's personal assistant, and one from Defendants' attorney. None of these declarations set forth any facts establishing that it would be too inconvenient for third party witnesses to travel to San Francisco. The Court declines to consider the declarations submitted for the first time in reply, as Defendants make no showing of why this evidence was not available at the time the moving papers were filed. (Jay v. Mahaffey (2013) 218 Cal.App.4th 1522, 1537, "The general rule of motion practice, which applies here, is that new evidence is not permitted with reply papers¿Defendants' decision to wait until the reply briefs to bring forth any evidence at all, when the [plaintiffs] would have no opportunity to respond, was simply unfair.") In any event, even if the Court were to consider the declarations submitted in reply, they would not change the result. The vast majority of the declarations are from Defendants' employees. Consideration is not given to the convenience of witnesses who are employees of the litigants. (Stute v. Burinda (1981) 123 Cal.App.3d Supp. 11, 105-106.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EHG)
